title: DOT Transport Regulations and X-ray Machine Requirements
narrationScript: Your license covers possessing and using a source. The moment the camera rolls onto a public road, the Department of Transportation owns the rules. This lesson walks that transport system end to end. Radioactive material is hazard class seven, and a working radiography source exceeds its A-one limit, so it travels in a Type B package — a design proven against a thirty-foot drop, a puncture bar, a fourteen-seventy-five-degree fire, and immersion. You will assign labels the way an inspector checks them — White one, Yellow two, or Yellow three — set by two measurements: the surface dose rate and the transport index at one meter. Yellow three aboard means placards on all four sides of the vehicle. You will build shipping papers, keep them within reach in the cab, secure the device against motion and against theft, and carry the required hazmat training. Then we cross to X-ray machines: registered with the state, never licensed by the NRC, FDA standards at the factory, and the same dose limits in the field. The switch changes. The discipline does not.

sections.heading: When the Source Hits the Road, DOT Takes Over
sections.body:
Your radioactive materials license — NRC or Agreement State — governs how you **possess and use** a source. The moment that source leaves the vault or the job site and rolls onto a public road, a second regulator takes over: the **U.S. Department of Transportation**, through the Hazardous Materials Regulations in **49 CFR Parts 171–178**. Radioactive material is **hazard Class 7**, one of the nine DOT hazard classes, and every radiography source on the highway is a Class 7 shipment — whether it rides with a common carrier or in your own crew truck.

The two agencies split the work under a long-standing memorandum of understanding:

- **DOT** writes the carriage rules — classification, package selection, marking, labeling, placarding, shipping papers, employee training, securement, and incident reporting.
- **NRC** certifies the design and performance of **Type B packages** under **10 CFR Part 71** and inspects the quality-assurance programs of the companies that use them.

One more role to keep straight: when your company hauls its own exposure devices, it is both the **shipper** and a **private carrier** — your crew carries both sets of duties, and the radiographer behind the wheel is personally executing federal transportation law.

sections.heading: Type A, Type B, and the A1 Line
sections.body:
DOT grades packaging to match the activity inside: small quantities move in **excepted** or **Type A** packages; large quantities demand **Type B**. The dividing line is the **A1 value** — the maximum activity of **special form** material (a high-integrity sealed capsule, certified against its own impact and heat tests) permitted in a Type A package. Each radionuclide has its own A1: for iridium-192 it is **1 TBq (27 Ci)**; for cobalt-60, **0.4 TBq (11 Ci)**.

Run the numbers on a working radiography source and the conclusion is automatic: a typical **100-curie Ir-192 source (3.7 TBq) is nearly four times its A1**, so it must travel in a **Type B package**.

- A **Type A** package (DOT Specification 7A is the workhorse) must survive **normal transport**: water spray, a roughly four-foot free drop, compression, and a penetration test.
- A **Type B** package must survive the **hypothetical accident sequence** shown in the figure — and afterward still contain its contents, keep its shielding, and read no more than **1 rem/h at 1 meter**.

Type B designs are certified by the NRC with a **Certificate of Compliance (CoC)**, and the package is durably marked with its certificate number — for example, USA/9296/B(U)-96 — plus the radiation trefoil. Users must be **registered** with the NRC for that package, operate it exactly as the certificate directs, and maintain a Part 71 **quality-assurance program**. For most field moves the certified Type B package *is* the exposure device itself, locked and fitted with its shipping plugs and covers per the CoC — and the same is true of the source changer when a decayed source goes back to the supplier.

sections.caption: The Type B hypothetical-accident sequence of 10 CFR 71: one package endures the 30-foot drop, the puncture bar, the 1,475 °F fire, and immersion — and must still contain its source, hold its shielding, and read no more than 1 rem/h at 1 meter.

sections.heading: Labels, the Transport Index, and Placards
sections.body:
Every Class 7 package wears one of three labels, and the label is earned by **measurement, not memory** — you survey the loaded package before every shipment. Two numbers decide it:

- the **maximum dose rate at the package surface**, and
- the **transport index (TI)** — the maximum dose rate in mrem/h at **1 meter** from the surface, rounded **up** to the next tenth.

**White-I** packages read no more than **0.5 mrem/h at the surface**. **Yellow-II** allows up to **50 mrem/h** at the surface with a **TI of 1 or less**. **Yellow-III** allows up to **200 mrem/h** at the surface with a **TI up to 10**. Exceed *either* number and the package moves up a category. Beyond the Yellow-III limits a shipment may move only as **exclusive use** — sole use of the vehicle by a single shipper — where the package surface may read up to 1,000 mrem/h in a closed vehicle, but the *vehicle* must still meet **200 mrem/h at its outer surface**, **10 mrem/h at 2 meters**, and **2 mrem/h in any normally occupied position** (private carriers may exceed the cab limit only when the exposed crew wear dosimetry under a radiation protection program).

Duplicate labels go on **two opposite sides** of the package, and Yellow labels list the radionuclide, the activity, and the TI. The package is also marked with its proper shipping name and identification number — for a radiography device, **UN 2916, Radioactive material, Type B(U) package**. The moment any **Yellow-III** package is aboard, the vehicle wears **RADIOACTIVE placards on all four sides**. A working camera typically lands in Yellow-II or Yellow-III — the survey meter, not habit, makes the call.

sections.caption: Two measurements assign the label — maximum surface dose rate and the transport index at 1 meter. Exceeding either limit pushes the package up a category, and any Yellow-III package aboard placards the vehicle on all four sides.

sections.heading: Shipping Papers and the Driver's Duties
sections.body:
Class 7 shipping papers carry a precise description in a fixed order — **identification number, proper shipping name, hazard class**: *UN 2916, Radioactive material, Type B(U) package, 7* — followed by the **radionuclide** (Ir-192), the words **special form**, the **activity** in terabecquerels (curies may follow in parentheses), the **label category**, the **TI**, and the **package certificate number**. Add a **24-hour emergency response telephone number** that is actually monitored, attach **emergency response information**, and sign the **shipper's certification** — a federal attestation that the shipment is prepared in full compliance.

On the road, the rules follow the driver:

- Papers stay **within immediate reach** while you are belted in — and go on the driver's seat or in the door pouch whenever you leave the cab.
- The package is **blocked and braced** so it cannot shift, and on a non-exclusive-use vehicle the transport indexes aboard may total **no more than 50**.
- Everyone who prepares or transports hazmat is a **hazmat employee**: trained within 90 days of assignment and **re-trained at least every three years** in general awareness, function-specific duties, safety, and security awareness.
- A **placarded** vehicle puts the driver in commercial territory: a **CDL with the hazardous-materials (H) endorsement**, which carries a TSA security screening.

Paperwork failures are the most common transport citation in radiography — and every one of them is discoverable at a roadside inspection.

sections.title: Parked is when sources get stolen
sections.body: Radiography's worst transport events are rarely crashes — they are **thefts of unattended trucks**, and stolen cameras have turned up in scrap yards with pry marks on the source tube. A working Ir-192 source above **0.8 TBq (21.6 Ci)** is a **Category 2 quantity**, so the security rules of **10 CFR Part 37** ride along: keep the shipment under **constant control and surveillance** during transit, with the means to summon help immediately. When the device is not under your direct watch, it sits behind **two independent physical controls** — for example, locked inside its secured transport compartment, inside a locked vehicle. Confirm arrival when a source ships by carrier, and treat a missing device as an emergency: lost or stolen licensed material is **immediately reportable** through your RSO to the regulator.

sections.heading: X-ray Machines: Registered, Not Licensed
sections.body:
The Atomic Energy Act gives the NRC authority over **byproduct, source, and special nuclear material** — and machine-produced radiation is none of those. An industrial X-ray tube is therefore **outside NRC jurisdiction entirely**. The regulator is your **state radiation control program**, and the instrument of control is **registration**: you notify the state of each machine (commonly within 30 days of acquisition), pay the fee, and submit to state inspection. Most states pattern their rules on the **CRCPD Suggested State Regulations**, so the field discipline mirrors source radiography:

- a designated **RSO** and written **operating and emergency procedures**
- **trained, certified operators** — many states require the **IRRSP X-ray** card or an equivalent
- **personnel dosimetry**, **calibrated survey instruments**, and conspicuous **postings and warning signals**
- the **same occupational dose limits**, adopted from the 10 CFR 20 framework — 5 rem per year does not care how the photon was made

Two federal agencies still touch machines. The **FDA**, under **21 CFR 1020**, sets performance standards for manufacturers — a certified **cabinet X-ray system**, for instance, may emit no more than **0.5 mR/h at 5 cm** from any external surface and must have interlocked doors and X-RAY ON indicators. **OSHA's** ionizing-radiation rule (29 CFR 1910.1096) backstops worker protection where no other rule reaches. And transport? A **de-energized tube emits nothing** — it ships as ordinary freight with no Class 7 controls at all. Your state's regulations and your registration conditions are the governing word; verify them before you energize.

sections.caption: Gamma sources and X-ray machines side by side — possession, transport, and equipment rules.
rows: Specific license from the NRC or an Agreement State (10 CFR 34 or state equivalent)
Registration with the state radiation control program — no NRC license exists for machines
Never off — the source emits continuously; only shielding and decay reduce it
Zero when de-energized — but prove it with a survey meter, not the panel light
Moving it on a public road
DOT hazard Class 7: Type B package, labels and TI, placards, shipping papers, trained driver
Ordinary freight — a de-energized tube is not radioactive material
Exposure device built to ANSI N432; package certified by the NRC under 10 CFR 71
FDA manufacturer performance standards (21 CFR 1020), with interlocks and X-RAY ON indicators
Certified radiographer (e.g., ASNT IRRSP, RAM category) plus employer training per 10 CFR 34.43
State-required training; many states call for the IRRSP X-ray category or equivalent
10 CFR 20 limits — 5 rem/yr TEDE and companions
The same limits, adopted in state machine regulations

figures.title: Type B package hypothetical-accident test sequence
Package labels, transport index, and placarding decision

interactive.title: Shipment Compliance Check
interactive.intro: You are the radiographer in charge, moving a 95-curie iridium-192 exposure device by company truck to a pipeline job. Your company is both shipper and private carrier — so federal transport law rides with you. Make the calls.

steps.prompt: Back at the shop you prepare the loaded device for the road. The package's label category and transport index are determined by:
options: The labels already on the case from the last job — categories don't change
A fresh survey of the loaded package: the maximum dose rate at the surface and at 1 meter, measured with a calibrated instrument
A decay calculation from the source certificate — paperwork, not measurement
The carrier — labeling is the driver's job, not the shipper's
steps.feedback: Labels are assigned from measured radiation levels on this package, this shipment. The surface reading and the 1-meter reading (the TI) move as the source decays, as sources are exchanged, and as shielding position changes — so you survey every time. The shipper assigns the category, and today the shipper is you.

steps.prompt: Your survey reads a maximum of 60 mrem/h at the surface and 1.8 mrem/h at 1 meter. The package is:
options: White-I — the device shield keeps it harmless
Yellow-II with TI = 1.8, because the TI is what really matters
Yellow-III with TI = 1.8 — the 60 mrem/h surface reading exceeds the Yellow-II surface limit of 50
Yellow-III with TI = 60, using the higher of the two readings
steps.feedback: A package must satisfy BOTH criteria of a category. The TI of 1.8 alone would only require Yellow-III anyway (TI > 1), and the 60 mrem/h surface reading independently busts the 50 mrem/h Yellow-II limit. The TI is always the 1-meter value — 1.8 — never the surface reading.

steps.prompt: The cased device, now wearing Yellow-III labels on two opposite sides, goes into the truck's device compartment. Before you pull out of the yard, the vehicle needs:
options: A RADIOACTIVE placard on the rear only — that's the side other drivers see
RADIOACTIVE placards on all four sides, shipping papers within your reach in the cab, and the package blocked, braced, and locked down
No placards — placards apply to common carriers, not company trucks
Placards only if the route crosses a state line
steps.feedback: Any package bearing a Yellow-III label placards the vehicle on all four sides — company truck or common carrier, around the block or across the country. Papers ride within immediate reach of the belted driver, and the package is secured against any shift. Placarded also means the driver needs a hazmat-endorsed CDL.

steps.prompt: Two hours into the drive you stop to eat. The legal way to leave a Category 2 source with the truck is:
options: Keep the truck under constant surveillance, or leave the source behind two independent physical controls — the locked, secured device compartment inside the locked vehicle
Lock the cab and carry the shipping papers in with you — papers prove ownership
Back the truck against a wall so the placards don't attract attention
Nothing special — stops under 30 minutes are exempt from security requirements
steps.feedback: 10 CFR Part 37 requires constant control and surveillance of a Category 2 shipment in transit — and when your eyes are off it, two independent physical barriers between a thief and the source. There is no short-stop exemption, hiding placards is itself a violation, and the papers stay with the vehicle (on the seat or in the door pouch) when the driver steps away.

checkQuestions.question: Who regulates a radiography source while it is being transported on a public highway?
options: The NRC alone — radioactive material is exempt from DOT rules
DOT, under the Hazardous Materials Regulations of 49 CFR, with the NRC certifying Type B package designs under 10 CFR 71
OSHA, because driving is a workplace activity
The EPA, under environmental release rules
checkQuestions.explanation: Possession and use fall under the NRC or Agreement State license, but transport in commerce belongs to DOT's 49 CFR Parts 171–178. The agencies share the job by memorandum of understanding: DOT writes the carriage rules; the NRC certifies Type B packaging performance.

checkQuestions.question: A typical 100-curie Ir-192 radiography source must be transported in a Type B package because:
options: All radioactive material, regardless of quantity, requires Type B packaging
Iridium-192 emits neutrons that ordinary packages cannot shield
Its activity exceeds the A1 special-form limit for Ir-192 — 1 TBq (27 Ci) — and above A1 the package must survive the hypothetical accident tests
DOT requires Type B for any shipment that crosses a state line
checkQuestions.explanation: A1 is the most special-form activity a Type A package may carry. For Ir-192 that line is 1 TBq (27 Ci); a 100 Ci working source is nearly four times over it, so it moves in an NRC-certified Type B package proven against the drop, puncture, fire, and immersion sequence.

checkQuestions.question: The transport index (TI) assigned to a package is:
options: The maximum dose rate in mrem/h at 1 meter from the external surface of the package, rounded up to the next tenth
The dose rate at the package surface divided by ten
The total activity in curies divided by the A1 value
The number of half-value layers in the package shielding
checkQuestions.explanation: The TI is a measured number — the highest reading at 1 meter from the package surface, expressed in mrem/h and rounded up to the first decimal. It drives label category (TI ≤ 1 for Yellow-II, ≤ 10 for Yellow-III) and the 50-TI cap on a non-exclusive-use vehicle.

checkQuestions.question: A RADIOACTIVE placard is required on the transport vehicle:
options: Whenever any quantity of radioactive material is aboard
Only when the total activity aboard exceeds the A2 value
Only for trips longer than 100 miles
Whenever any package bearing a RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III label is aboard — displayed on all four sides of the vehicle
checkQuestions.explanation: White-I and Yellow-II packages do not by themselves placard a vehicle; a Yellow-III package does, and the placards go on each side and each end. Since a working radiography camera commonly rates Yellow-III, the crew truck is commonly a placarded vehicle — which also triggers the CDL hazmat endorsement.

checkQuestions.question: Industrial X-ray machines used for radiography are regulated primarily by:
options: The NRC, through a specific license under 10 CFR 34
State radiation control programs through machine registration, with FDA performance standards applying to manufacturers
DOT, because an X-ray machine is hazard Class 7 in transit
No one — machine-produced radiation is unregulated
checkQuestions.explanation: Machine-produced radiation is not byproduct, source, or special nuclear material, so the NRC has no jurisdiction. States register and inspect X-ray machines under rules patterned on the CRCPD Suggested State Regulations, the FDA sets manufacturer standards in 21 CFR 1020, and the dose limits match the 10 CFR 20 framework.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Appendix A §12.7–12.8 (DOT regulations for radiographic source shipment; regulatory requirements for X-ray machines)
49 CFR Parts 171–178 — DOT Hazardous Materials Regulations (Class 7: §172.403 labels, §172.504 placarding, §173.435 A1/A2 values, §173.441 radiation limits, §172.704 training)
10 CFR Part 71 — Packaging and Transportation of Radioactive Material (Type B certification and quality assurance); 10 CFR Part 37 (security of Category 2 quantities in transit)
FDA 21 CFR 1020 and CRCPD Suggested State Regulations (X-ray machine performance standards and state registration requirements)
